metadata.subject: Your dead hang protocol starts now
metadata.body:
Welcome to the dead hang protocol.

Here's your **Week 1** prescription:

**3 rounds x 10 seconds | 60 seconds rest**

That's 30 seconds of total hang time. Do it daily.

If 10 seconds feels easy, good — we're building tendon tolerance first. Tendons adapt 3-6x slower than muscle (Magnusson et al., J Physiol, 2010). Rushing this is how you get medial epicondylitis.

### Form checklist
- Overhand grip, thumbs wrapped
- Shoulders relaxed (passive hang) — let your bodyweight do the work
- Feet off the ground, no kipping
- Breathe normally

Track your longest unbroken hang today. That's your baseline.

See you in 7 days.

— [deadhangs.com](https://deadhangs.com)

metadata.subject: Week 2: Add 5 seconds
metadata.body:
Week 1 done. Here's Week 2:

**3 rounds x 15 seconds | 60 seconds rest**

You should feel the difference in your forearm extensors already. Grip endurance typically improves 12-18% in the first two weeks of consistent hanging (Bohannon, Clin Interv Aging, 2019).

### This week's focus: Scapular awareness
During your hang, notice whether your shoulders are by your ears (passive) or slightly pulled down (active). Both are useful:

- **Passive hang** — decompresses the spine, stretches lats and pecs
- **Active hang** — engages lower traps and serratus anterior, builds shoulder stability

Alternate between them. 2 rounds passive, 1 round active.

— [deadhangs.com](https://deadhangs.com)

metadata.subject: Week 3: Your grip vs. your mortality risk
metadata.body:
Week 3 protocol:

**3 rounds x 20 seconds | 60 seconds rest**

You're now hanging for a full minute per session. Here's why that matters:

In 2015, the PURE study tracked 139,691 adults across 17 countries. They found each 5 kg decrease in grip strength was associated with a 16% increase in all-cause mortality and a 17% increase in cardiovascular mortality (Leong et al., Lancet, 2015).

Grip strength outperformed systolic blood pressure as a predictor of death.

You're not just training your forearms. You're training a biomarker.

— [deadhangs.com](https://deadhangs.com)

metadata.subject: Week 4: The plateau is normal
metadata.body:
Week 4 protocol:

**3 rounds x 25 seconds | 75 seconds rest**

If your hang time hasn't improved much this week, that's expected. Neural adaptations (better motor unit recruitment) dominate weeks 1-3. Structural adaptations — tendon collagen remodelling, muscle fibre hypertrophy — take longer.

### Quick self-test
Hang from the bar with just your fingertips (open grip, no thumb wrap). If you can hold 5+ seconds, your finger flexors are adapting well. If not, keep training — you're building the foundation.

Your rest periods increased to 75 seconds this week. Use them fully.

— [deadhangs.com](https://deadhangs.com)

metadata.subject: Month 1 done — here's your checkpoint
metadata.body:
Week 5 protocol:

**4 rounds x 20 seconds | 60 seconds rest**

We're adding a round and resetting the duration. Total hang time stays similar but you get more practice at the grip-endurance threshold.

### Month 1 benchmarks
By now you should notice:
- Callus formation on the proximal finger pads
- Reduced forearm soreness after sessions
- Easier time opening jars, carrying groceries (real-world grip transfer)
- Some reduction in upper back / shoulder tightness

If your baseline was 10 seconds and you can now hold 20+, you're tracking well.

— [deadhangs.com](https://deadhangs.com)

metadata.subject: Week 6: Spinal decompression — what the research says
metadata.body:
Week 6 protocol:

**4 rounds x 25 seconds | 60 seconds rest**

Orthopaedic surgeon John Kirsch has documented improvements in shoulder impingement symptoms through regular overhead hanging protocols. His hypothesis: hanging restores the subacromial space by reshaping the acromion and coracoacromial ligament over time.

### The decompression angle
Axial loading from sitting compresses intervertebral discs throughout the day. Hanging creates traction in the opposite direction. While research on disc rehydration from hanging specifically is limited, spinal traction has established evidence for reducing intradiscal pressure (Pellecchia, J Orthop Sports Phys Ther, 1994).

Hang after your workday. Your spine will thank you.

— [deadhangs.com](https://deadhangs.com)

metadata.subject: Week 7: The halfway point
metadata.body:
Week 7 protocol:

**4 rounds x 30 seconds | 75 seconds rest**

You're at the halfway mark. Time for a max test.

### Max hang test
Warm up with 2 x 15 second hangs. Rest 2 minutes. Then hang as long as you can. Record the time.

**Age-adjusted benchmarks (healthy adults):**

| Age | Below avg | Average | Good | Excellent |
|-----|-----------|---------|------|-----------|
| 20-29 | < 30s | 30-60s | 60-90s | 90s+ |
| 30-39 | < 25s | 25-50s | 50-75s | 75s+ |
| 40-49 | < 20s | 20-40s | 40-60s | 60s+ |
| 50-59 | < 15s | 15-30s | 30-50s | 50s+ |
| 60+ | < 10s | 10-20s | 20-40s | 40s+ |

*Benchmarks adapted from normative grip endurance data (Bohannon, 2019). Individual variation is significant.*

— [deadhangs.com](https://deadhangs.com)

metadata.subject: Week 8: Variations to break through
metadata.body:
Week 8 protocol:

**4 rounds x 30 seconds | 60 seconds rest**
- Round 1-2: Overhand grip (pronated)
- Round 3: Underhand grip (supinated)
- Round 4: Mixed grip (one hand over, one under)

Grip variation changes which forearm muscles are primarily loaded:
- **Overhand** — flexor digitorum profundus + superficialis
- **Underhand** — adds biceps brachii and brachialis as stabilisers
- **Mixed** — trains anti-rotation through the torso

Variety prevents overuse patterns and builds more complete forearm development.

— [deadhangs.com](https://deadhangs.com)

metadata.subject: Week 9: Why grip strength declines with age
metadata.body:
Week 9 protocol:

**4 rounds x 35 seconds | 75 seconds rest**

Skeletal muscle mass declines approximately 3-8% per decade after age 30, accelerating after 60 (Volpi et al., Curr Opin Clin Nutr Metab Care, 2004). Grip strength follows the same curve.

This is sarcopenia — the age-related loss of muscle mass and function. It's the reason falls become dangerous. It's the reason getting out of a chair becomes hard.

Grip strength is one of the earliest measurable markers of sarcopenia. And unlike a DEXA scan, you can test it with a pull-up bar.

You are currently reversing your personal curve. Keep hanging.

— [deadhangs.com](https://deadhangs.com)

metadata.subject: Week 10: Building the habit that lasts
metadata.body:
Week 10 protocol:

**4 rounds x 35 seconds | 60 seconds rest**

The protocol ends in two weeks but the habit shouldn't. Research on habit formation suggests an average of 66 days for automaticity (Lally et al., Eur J Soc Psychol, 2010) — you're past that threshold now.

### Post-protocol maintenance
After Week 12, you have three options:

1. **Maintain** — 3 x 30s daily (5 minutes, preserves your gains)
2. **Progress** — Add weight via a dip belt (start with 5 kg / 10 lb)
3. **Specialise** — One-arm hang progressions, towel hangs, fat grip hangs

Most people do best with option 1 on weekdays and option 2-3 on weekends.

— [deadhangs.com](https://deadhangs.com)

metadata.subject: Week 11: One more week
metadata.body:
Week 11 protocol:

**5 rounds x 30 seconds | 60 seconds rest**

Five rounds this week — your highest volume yet. Total hang time: 2 minutes 30 seconds.

### Max test #2
Same protocol as Week 7: warm up with 2 x 15s, rest 2 minutes, then max hang.

Compare to your Week 7 result. Typical improvement over 4 weeks at this stage is 15-30%.

Write it down. This is your data.

— [deadhangs.com](https://deadhangs.com)

metadata.subject: Week 12: Protocol complete
metadata.body:
Final week protocol:

**5 rounds x 35 seconds | 75 seconds rest**

Total hang time: 2 minutes 55 seconds. You started at 30 seconds in Week 1.

### What 12 weeks built
- Grip endurance: likely 2-3x your starting baseline
- Tendon resilience: collagen remodelling peaks around 12 weeks of consistent loading
- A longevity biomarker: grip strength is associated with reduced all-cause and cardiovascular mortality (Leong et al., Lancet, 2015)
- Spinal decompression habit: daily traction counteracting sitting compression
- Shoulder mobility: overhead hanging range maintained or improved

### What's next
The [dead hang protocol page](https://deadhangs.com) has the calculator and timer for your next phase. Bookmark it.

Keep hanging.

— [deadhangs.com](https://deadhangs.com)
